title: Solar System Studio
description: A compact educational dataset for an interactive object-lab introduction to the eight planets.
source_notes: Planet order, diameters, average distance from the Sun, and orbital periods are rounded from NASA planetary fact sheets.

The visual model is intentionally compressed. Orbital spacing and planet sizes are not rendered to the same scale at the same time because the real ratios are too extreme for a readable classroom interface.

planets.name: Mercury
planets.surface: Cratered rock and wide temperature swings
planets.systemRole: Inner boundary marker for how intense sunlight becomes close to the Sun.
facts: Mercury completes one orbit in only 88 Earth days.
Its lack of a thick atmosphere means heat is not spread around the planet.
It is larger than Earth's Moon, but smaller than every other planet.

planets.name: Venus
planets.systemRole: A warning label for greenhouse heating and atmospheric pressure.
facts: Venus is close to Earth's size, but its surface environment is radically different.
It rotates backward relative to most planets.
A Venus day is longer than a Venus year.

planets.name: Earth
planets.tagline: The ocean world we use as the ruler.
planets.surface: Liquid water, continents, ice, and active geology
planets.systemRole: The reference point for distance, habitability, and scale.
facts: Earth is the only known planet with long-lived surface oceans.
Its Moon stabilizes tides and gives the Earth-Moon system unusual visual presence.
One astronomical unit is based on Earth's average distance from the Sun.

planets.name: Mars
planets.tagline: A cold desert with old river clues.
planets.surface: Dusty basalt, volcanoes, canyons, polar caps
planets.systemRole: The outer rocky planet and the most reachable world for surface exploration.
planets.moons: Two small moons, Phobos and Deimos
facts: Mars has a day length very close to Earth's.
Its year is almost twice as long as Earth's.
Surface features preserve evidence that liquid water once shaped parts of the planet.

planets.name: Jupiter
planets.surface: No solid surface; deep atmosphere and metallic hydrogen interior
planets.systemRole: Dominant planet by mass, sculpting small-body orbits and holding a large moon system.
planets.moons: Large moon system including Io, Europa, Ganymede, and Callisto
facts: Jupiter's diameter is more than eleven times Earth's.
It spins very quickly, making one day less than ten hours long.
The Galilean moons are worlds in their own right.

planets.name: Saturn
planets.tagline: A ring system you can see as architecture.
planets.surface: No solid surface; layered atmosphere and deep interior
planets.systemRole: The clearest example that a planet can be a system: rings, moons, gaps, and resonances.
planets.moons: Large moon system including Titan and Enceladus
facts: Saturn's rings are broad, thin, and made mostly of icy particles.
Titan has a dense atmosphere and liquid hydrocarbon lakes.
Saturn is less dense than water on average.

planets.name: Uranus
planets.surface: No solid surface; icy mantle beneath atmosphere
planets.systemRole: Shows how collisions and tilt can rewrite a planet's seasons.
planets.moons: A system of icy moons and faint rings
facts: Uranus rotates on its side, with an axial tilt near 98 degrees.
Methane in the atmosphere helps create its blue-green color.
Its long year makes seasons last for decades.

planets.name: Neptune
planets.tagline: The outer planet with fierce weather.
planets.surface: No solid surface; icy mantle beneath atmosphere
planets.systemRole: The far giant that marks the outer edge of the classical planet set.
facts: Neptune takes about 164 Earth years to orbit the Sun.
Its winds are among the fastest measured in the solar system.
Triton orbits in the opposite direction of Neptune's rotation, hinting at capture.
